coredata.dc:description: Antibacterial hydrogels have received attention for preventing infections and for their biomedical applications. However, traditional antibiotics-containing and metal nanoparticle-containing hydrogels often cause bacterial resistance, exhibit low biocompatibility, and lack real-time monitoring capability. Here, a fluorescent antibacterial hydrogel with antibacterial ability, excellent optical performance, and high biocompatibility was developed based on cationic carbon dots (CDs), pectin, and acrylic acid triggered construction of the hydrogel network by cross-linker. The antibacterial high-cationic CDs (+51.20 mV) were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method and released from hydrogel in response to broken hydrogen bonds due to a change in the ambient environment caused by the growing bacteria. The hydrogel showed long-term potent broad-spectrum antibacterial ability (even drug-resistant bacteria) due to the bacterial membrane seriously damaged by the released CDs. The inhibitory capability of this hydrogel was 108.5-fold higher than the other hydrogel. After implantation or incubation with cells, no obvious cytotoxicity or tissue toxicity was observed for the antibacterial hydrogel. This hydrogel enhanced both the application of CDs in vivo and the biosafety of hydrogel. Furthermore, the multicolor fluorescence emission produced by CD provides a potential idea for the development of dual-function hydrogels with in situ monitoring and prevention of bacterial infections to treat wounds.

Assembly of the fluorescent antibacterial CDs hydrogel through pectin, acrylic acid, CDs, APS, and PEGDA. C. Scheme of molecular structure and applications of fluorescent antibacterial hydrogel. a) cell adhesion and biocompatibility, b) antibacterial, c) and d) molecular structure of hydrogel. head.ce:title: Carbon dots-releasing hydrogels with antibacterial activity, high biocompatibility, and fluorescence performance as candidate materials for wound healing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center for Functional Food, School of Food Science Synergetic Innovation Center of Food Safety and Nutrition, Jiangnan University, Jiangsu, Wuxi 214122, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibacterial hydrogels have received attention for preventing infections and for their biomedical applications. However, traditional antibiotics-containing and metal nanoparticle-containing hydrogels often cause bacterial resistance, exhibit low biocompatibility, and lack real-time monitoring capability. Here, a fluorescent antibacterial hydrogel with antibacterial ability, excellent optical performance, and high biocompatibility was developed based on cationic carbon dots (CDs), pectin, and acrylic acid triggered construction of the hydrogel network by cross-linker. The antibacterial high-cationic CDs (+51.20 mV) were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method and released from hydrogel in response to broken hydrogen bonds due to a change in the ambient environment caused by the growing bacteria. The hydrogel showed long-term potent broad-spectrum antibacterial ability (even drug-resistant bacteria) due to the bacterial membrane seriously damaged by the released CDs. The inhibitory capability of this hydrogel was 108.5-fold higher than the other hydrogel. After implantation or incubation with cells, no obvious cytotoxicity or tissue toxicity was observed for the antibacterial hydrogel. This hydrogel enhanced both the application of CDs in vivo and the biosafety of hydrogel. Furthermore, the multicolor fluorescence emission produced by CD provides a potential idea for the development of dual-function hydrogels with in situ monitoring and prevention of bacterial infections to treat wounds.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections are daunting and pose a serious threat to modern health care (Dai et al., 2018). Every year, millions of people die of bacterial infections (Ji et al., 2016). Penicillin was first discovered in 1928, and various antibiotics are now widely used (Tan and Tatsumura, 2015). However, the indiscriminate use of antibiotics in medicine and agriculture has led to bacterial resistance (Gupta et al., 2018) and short-term antibacterial activity. In addition, a variety of drug-resistant bacteria and even “superbugs” (Konai et al., 2020) have emerged, which undoubtedly adds to the burden of treating infections. Unfortunately, bacterial resistance is growing much faster than the speed of discovering new antibiotics. High-efficiency disinfectants, such as benzalkonium chloride, triclosan, and triclocarban, have been forced to be candidates for alternative antibiotics (Li et al., 2016). However, the use of disinfectants can lead to health complications, strong irritation, and biological toxicity (Landecker, 2019). Various advanced antibacterial materials have been studied to overcome this problem, such as polymers (Shuai et al., 2019), metal nanomaterials (Wang et al., 2019; Xin et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019), natural extracts (Nonejuie et al., 2016) and membranes (Wang et al., 2017). However, while these materials inhibit and kill pathogenic bacteria, they also have a toxic effect on normal cells, which limits their potential applications. Therefore, more effective antibacterial agents for treatment of infection are needed.

Hydrogels are three-dimensional porous materials composed of polymer chains with physical or chemical cross-links (Qu et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2017). Due to their similarity to biological tissues, they have been extensively researched in medicine and tissue engineering (Seliktar, 2012; Yuk et al., 2019). Recent research on antibacterial hydrogels has opened up new ways to combat bacterial infectious diseases owing to the structural diversity, high oxygen permeability, and high water distensibility of hydrogels (Gan et al., 2019b). Hydrogels with different properties, such as hydrogels with different porosities and hydrophilicities, can be synthesized for antibacterial applications by screening and matching monomers and crosslinking agents. Antibacterial hydrogels use the hydrogel as a support carrier, and the antibacterial purpose is achieved by immobilizing antibiotics (Yu et al., 2019, 2020). Hoque et al., (2018) employed dual-function injectable hydrogels to deliver vancomycin locally to treat vascular or necrotic tissue infections. Wu et al., (2014) presented a gentamycin sulfate-loaded hydrogel with genipin cross-linked carboxymethyl-chitosan, which not only inhibited the growth of Staphylococcus aureus and the formation of biofilm but also improved the proliferation, adhesion, and differentiation of mammalian cells. However, bacterial resistance was triggered due to excessive use of antibiotics. Some antibacterial hydrogels are incorporated with antibacterial nanomaterials, such as silver and gold nanoparticles, to prevent bacterial infection. Li et al., (2019) synthesized a lignin-based antimicrobial hydrogel by incorporating silver nanoparticles to enhance the antimicrobial effect. Ribeiro et al., (2017) prepared silk nanohydroxyapatite/fibroin hydrogels with synthesized gold and silver nanoparticles in situ, which revealed significant inhibitory effects against bacteria. However, some metal nanoparticles might be cytotoxic. Therefore, biocompatibility and preventing bacterial drug-resistant induction of bactericidal agents incorporated in hydrogels are major concerns.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted interest due to their optical and fluorescent properties, easy surface functionalization, biocompatibility, extremely small size, good water dispersibility, and easy synthetic routes from readily available materials, and have been widely used in sensing (Liu et al., 2019b), biological imaging (Zhang et al., 2019), optical devices (Liu et al., 2019a), photocatalysts (Zhou et al., 2019) and photodynamic therapy (Xu et al., 2020). Studies on antibacterial CDs have also recently emerged (Jijie et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018). The cationic antibacterial mechanism is the most common mechanism to sterilize CDs (Lin et al., 2019). Due to the positive charge and small size, sterilization seriously damages the bacterial membrane and causes cell death (Jian et al., 2017; Travlou et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019). Yang and coworkers prepared positively charged CDs with quaternary ammonium groups on the surface, these CDs had simultaneous antibacterial and bacterial differentiation capabilities (Yang et al., 2016). Ran et al., (2019) reported strong positively charged CDs with long alkyl chain-linked quaternary ammonium groups on the surface for imaging and eliminating bacterial biofilms. Using immobilized antibacterial cationic CDs, may be an effective way to develop immobilized highly biocompatible antimicrobial hydrogels with no bacterial drug resistance. Importantly, immobilizing CDs within the hydrogel supramolecular framework has been pursued, and CD fluorescent hydrogels have been widely synthesized for sensing fluorescent materials (Wei et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2018). Bhattacharya et al., (2019) prepared fluorescent self-healing CD/polymer gels using the Schiff base reaction. This is a great reference for immobilizing antibacterial CDs into a hydrogel.

Although CDs have been incorporated into hydrogels as guests, the use of CDs as a component of an actual antibacterial hydrogel has not been reported. Here, a cationic CD hydrogel with antibacterial ability, excellent optical performance, good biocompatibility, and cell adhesiveness was developed. Cationic CDs were first prepared using various precursors and a hydrothermal synthetic route to synthesize the cationic CD antibacterial hydrogel ( Fig. 1A). Then, acrylic acid (AA), pectin, cationic CDs, ammonium persulfate (APS), and poly (ethylene glycol) dimethacrylate (PEGDA) were penetrated into the three-dimensional copolymerization network to form spermidine-CDs with AA and pectin (SCDs-AP) hydrogel using a one-pot method (Fig. 1B). The hydrogel network was constructed by forming ester bonds between polyacrylic acid and pectin, and the reaction between the amino groups on the surface of the CDs and the carboxyl groups of polyacrylic acid and pectin generated hydrogen bonds, and finally formed a hydrogel. Importantly, the CDs played a skeletal adhesion role in the hydrogel network, acted as fluorophores, and were released to play an antibacterial role (Fig. 1C). Unlike previously reported nanoparticle- or antibiotic-incorporated antibacterial hydrogels, some outstanding advantages of these hydrogels are as follows. First, the prepared hydrogels exhibited super active antibacterial activity. When bacterial cells came in contact with the hydrogel, the dynamic balance of the hydrogen bonds was broken due to changes in the surrounding electrical charge. The cationic CDs were released and the bacterial cells were killed without inducing bacterial resistance. Second, the hydrogels displayed both good fluorescent properties and good recoverability. Due to electrostatic repulsion and hydrogen bonding, the CDs were uniformly dispersed throughout the gel network. The CDs as fluorophores determined the overall emission color of the hydrogel. APS was used as an initiator for the polymerization or copolymerization of the water-soluble monomer acrylic acid. PEGDA is a cross-linking agent used to cross-link the polyacrylic acid and pectin chains with chemical crosslinks. CDs incorporated into the hydrogel formed physical crosslinks with the PAA and pectin chains. Chemical and physical synergistic crosslinking imparted good recoverability to the hydrogel. Finally, the hydrogels displayed high biocompatibility and cell affinity due to the amine groups and hydrogen bonding of the CDs. In short, a novel bactericidal mechanism for a contact-active antibacterial hydrogel was studied. This hydrogel may be suitable for protecting wounds from bacterial infections and real-time observations in vivo. The hydrogel is expected to be applied in the field of biomedical or safety detection.

Reagent grades of o-phenylenediamine, ammonium persulfate (APS, 98.0%), α-lipoic acid, poly (ethylene glycol) dimethacrylate (PEGDA, average Mn 700) were purchased from Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Kanamycin was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Spermidine (99.0%), pectin (galacturonic acid content ≥ 74.0%) and acrylic acid (AA, 99.0%) were supplied by Macklin. The L929 fibroblast cells were from the Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Minimum Eagle’s medium (MEM), phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were from Gibco Invitrogen Corporation (CA, USA). All other chemicals were analytical grade.

CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal synthesis method (Cui et al., 2019a, 2019b). O-phenylenediamines, o-phenylenediamines/spermidine (at a weight ratio of 1:1) and o-phenylenediamines/α-lipoic acid (at a weight ratio of 1:1) were firstly dissolved in H2O at different concentrations (10 mg/mL) and 30 mL solutions were transferred into poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves (50 mL) to synthesized CDs, spermidine-CDs (SCDs) and lipoic acid-CDs (LCDs), respectively. Heating them with oven at 220 °C and 3 h, after naturally cooling down to room temperature, the suspensions were collected. With 12,000 rpm centrifugation for 15 min, the suspended CDs solution was passed through a filter (0.22 µm). The resulting CDs solution was dialyzed against deionized water in a 500 Da dialysis bag for 5 h, changing the dialysate every 1 h. Finally, the as-prepared CDs were stored at 4 °C and vacuum freeze-drying to use.

Pectin, AA, APS, PEGDA and various of CDs (1 mg/mL) were dissolved in the beaker and stirred to form a homogeneous solution. Transfer it into a mold, and then the sample was heated at 40 ℃ to form the AA-Pectin (AP) Hydrogel, SCDs-AA-Pectin (SCDs-AP) Hydrogel, CDs-AA-Pectin (CDs-AP) Hydrogel, and LCDs-AA-Pectin (LCDs-AP) Hydrogel. The details of preparation various hydrogels were listed in Table 1.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CDs were obtained on a JEM-2100, electron microscope operating at 200 kV (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of CDs were recorded with a KRATOS XSAM800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Ltd, Manchester, United Kingdom), using Mg as the exciting source. UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs were recorded on a Lambda EZ201 UV/vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Excitation and emission spectra of CDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi). 1 mL of three CDs solutions were added to 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes and placed in a dark chamber. Uv-light at 365 nm was used to illuminate the CDs and the luminescence of CDs was photographed. The whole experiment was completed within 1 min. The fluorescence quantum yields were measured using FL3-111 (Horiba Scientific, Edison, NJ). The morphological structure of hydrogels was examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM; JSM 6390, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). Before examination, the hydrogels were freeze-dried in vacuum at − 50 °C for the interior morphological investigation. The hardness, cohesiveness, resilience, and springiness tests were performed on a texture analyzer (TA-XTplus, Stable Micro Systems, Surrey, U.K.) (Qin et al., 2019). For the mechanical properties testing, the samples were of 20 mm width and 20 mm thickness. Afterward, the sample was placed on the lower plate of the instrument and equipped with a cylindrical P/0.5 probe. The depression distance is 50% of the hydrogel height, the trigger force is 5 g, the speed before and after the test is 2 mm/s, the test speed is 1 mm/s (Yu et al., 2017).

The gravimetric change of hydrogel at 37 °C over time were used to characterize the swelling capacity of hydrogels. The initial weight of the hydrogel sample was first accurately weighed and recorded (M0). The hydrogel was then placed in a PBS solution at 37 °C to observe the swelling change of the hydrogel, and the weight of the sample was recorded every hour until the sample reached the swelling equilibrium and the weight did not change. During the swelling measurement, the surface of the hydrogel should be wiped with a clean filter paper and weighed. Four replicates per group. Using the following equation to calculate the swelling ratio (Q) of the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vitro antibacterial activity assay of CDs and Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), Salmonella typhimurium ATCC 14028 (S. typhimurium), Aeromonas hydrophila (A. hydrophila), Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 29213 (S. aureus), Yersinia enterocolitica (Y. enterocolitica), Pseudomonas fluorescens (P. fluorescens), methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) and Multidrug-Resistant Salmonella Typhimurium (MRST) were stored in our lab (Cui et al., 2019a). All the bacteria strains were incubated in Luria-Bertani (LB) media, each single colony strain was picked from the LB ager plates and inoculated with shaking at 200 rpm (37 °C) until an OD600 of 1.0. Culture medium were centrifuged at 6000 rpm 10 min to collect the bacteria cells, and washed twice at room temperature prior to further use. Broth microdilution method was utilized to measure the MICs of the CDs against different strains (Jian et al., 2017; Li et al., 2016). The bacterial strains (104 CFU mL−1) were cultured with CDs in PBS at 37 °C under 250 rpm shaking for 3 h. After incubation, 100 μL from the bacterial suspension was deposited on the surface of the LB ager plate and cultured. The MIC values of CDs were also recorded. Each group of three parallel.

The four groups of hydrogels required for in vivo bacterial experiments and in vitro cell experiments were pretreated as follows: first, the prepared hydrogel was repeatedly immersed in deionized water and 75% ethanol solution to remove unreacted monomer, crosslinker, initiator and other impurities. Next, the hydrogel was immersed in a 75% ethanol solution for 24 h to sterilize. The sterilized hydrogel was then transferred to a sterile PBS solution in a clean bench for soaking and rinsing a plurality of times to remove the ethanol, and the measurement was performed to determine the pH of the soaking solution to be neutral. Finally, the hydrogel was transferred to a 24-well cell culture plate for use.

AP (Control), LCDs-AP, CDs-AP, and SCDs-AP hydrogels were as experimental groups in the antibacterial tests, AP was as control group. Each group of four parallel samples was utilized for antimicrobial testing. The bacterial strains were adjusted to 104 CFU mL−1 with PBS. Then 50 μL bacteria were coating on the LB ager plate, hydrogels were placed on the plate to antibacterial. All the plates were cultured for 12 h at 37 °C. For the different volume (50, 75, and 100 μL before formatted hydrogel) of SCDs-AP hydrogels were putted into a 24-well plate, then 100 μL of different concentrations of bacterial suspension (106, 107, 108, 109 CFU/mL) was dropped on the surface. All samples were cultured for 12 h at 37 °C. Next, 900 μL of cultural media was added and further cultured for 12 h at 37 °C. Ultimately, transferring 200 μL suspension of each sample into a 96-well plate. Bacterial growth was recorded by testing the optical density (OD) at 600 nm with a microplate reader (MQX200, BioTek, USA). Using the following equation to calculate the hydrogel bactericidal ratio (BR).
Percentage
of
BR =
control
group
OD − experimental

× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM and TEM images for bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: AP and SCDs-AP hydrogels were selected as the experimental groups in this test, untreated bacteria group was as control, MRSA and MRST bacteria were cultured in LB media at 37 °C with shaking at 200 rpm until an OD600 of 1.0. The bacterial strains were centrifuged and washed twice with PBS. Then the concentration of bacteria was adjusted to 106 CFU mL−1 with PBS. 1 mL bacteria suspensions were incubated with hydrogels for 3 h at 37 °C. After incubation, take out the hydrogels and centrifuge the bacteria culture at 6,000 rpm for 5 min. The pellet was washed twice with PBS and fixed using 2.5% glutaraldehyde in PBS. The sample was followed with vacuum drying and observed TEM and SEM images. For SEM samples need coat with gold by sputtering.

The release characteristics of the CD loaded with hydrogel were measured. Add the sample to a vial, and 1 mL of PBS solution was added, the CDs release from the hydrogel in a shaker at 100 rpm 37 °C. Then, the release liquid was taken out at a predetermined time (6 h, 12 h, 1d, 3d, 5d, 7d, 15d, 21d). Take the release solution: Take the original 0.5 mL of the release solution at the specified time point, then re-add 0.5 mL of new PBS solution to continue the release. Four replicates per group. Finally, the release solution of CDs was detected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi), and the content was calculated based on a standard curve established in advance (To avoid the fluorescent quenching effect of high CD concentration, the maximum concentration of the standard curve was 500 μg/mL). 10 μL of sample was diluted to 500 μL to detect the fluorescent intensity. The tendency of CD release from hydrogels was described by cumulative release.

To investigate the effect of pH to the release of CDs from hydrogel. 75 μL hydrogel was added in a vial, and add 1 mL of PBS (pH = 5.5, 6.0, 7.4, 8.0, 8.5) into the vial. The vials were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and test the fluorescent intensity using the above method. Four replicates per group.

The qualitative cell morphology was used to evaluate the proliferation and attachment of L929 fibroblast on the hydrogel surface, and the cytotoxicity was calculated by CCK-8 method. Hydrogels are prepared as mentioned above. The prepared hydrogels were placed in a 24-well plate with 4 parallel samples for each group of samples. No hydrogel group was the control group. The number of cells inoculated on each sample was 1 × 105, and the medium used was MEM supplemented with 10% FBS. After inoculation, 1 mL corresponding medium was added, the cultural medium was changed every day. The cells could adhere and grow for 3 days, at which time the cell activity was detected by CCK-8 assay. The CCK-8 assay was performed.

In this study, the antibacterial characteristics of AP (Control) and SCDs-AP hydrogels were evaluated in a rabbit subcutaneous model. The hydrogels were subcutaneously implanted on the back of four New Zealand rabbits (Baiyou, Shanghai, China, weighing 2–3 kg, male), following which 1 mL of S. aureus (105 cells/mL) was injected to the hydrogel site. The animal activity and appearance of the wound are checked daily during the day after surgery. Observe the wound at the surgical site. Rabbits were housed individually and freely access to food and water. Animals were euthanized one week after surgery. The entire sample was collected from each pocket. Specimens were placed in 10% formalin and embedded in paraffin blocks. Sections of tissue were fixed to slides, and Hematoxylin and Eosin were used to stained them.

All animal procedures were performed in accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals the National Guidelines for Experimental Animal Welfare (MOST of PR China, 2006) and approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Jiangnan University.

Student's t-test was conducted to determine the significant difference between groups. All data were evaluated as the mean ± standard deviation. Results with P values of less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant (***represents p < 0.001, **represents p < 0.01, and *represents p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and La-Y-CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis using o-phenylenediamines, o-phenylenediamines/spermidine, and o-phenylenediamines/α-lipoic acid at 220 °C for 3 h, respectively. After purifying the CDs through centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis, the yields of the Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and La-Y-CDs were 28.4%, 30.5%, and 14.9%, respectively. Fig. 2A–C showed the optical properties of the La-Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and Y-CDs, respectively. The best emission peaks of the La-Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and Y-CDs were 600, 430, and 575 nm, and the corresponding best excitation peaks were 400, 320, and 420 nm. The UV–vis absorption of La-Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and Y-CDs was also investigated. All three CDs had a typical absorption peak (the π–π* transition of the graphite sp2 domain) at 240 nm, and the n–π* absorption band (C–N) of the three CDs was also observed at 290 nm. A new La-Y-CD absorption peak was observed at 400 nm resulting in red emissions (Jiang et al., 2020a). The fluorescence quantum yields of La-Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and Y-CDs were 0.36%, 3.82%, and 2.15%, respectively.

The photoluminescence properties of CDs make them have excellent optical properties, which can be used in biological imaging (Du et al., 2019), fluorescence detection (Yue et al., 2019), optical devices (Liu et al., 2019a), photodynamic therapy (Xu et al., 2020), photocatalysis (Zhou et al., 2019) and other fields (Kang and Lee, 2019). At the same time, CDs with different fluorescence emission can be used in different fluorescence imaging and detection fields. For example, CDs with red fluorescence emission can be used for in vivo fluorescence imaging and detection applications due to their low biological background interference and high penetration ability (Hua et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2020b; Malina et al., 2019).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the three CD morphologies. Fig. 2D–F showed the three CD images, with a size distribution of 5−10 nm, and average diameters of 9.68 ± 0.21, 5.02 ± 0.43, and 7.80 ± 0.68 nm for the La-Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and Y-CDs, respectively. The microstructure of the CDs was explored by high-resolution TEM images. The Fig. 2D–F insets showed that La-Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and Y-CDs had identical lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, which was the same as the (102) diffractive surface of sp2 graphitic carbon, which adapts a diamond-like (sp3) diffractive surface of carbon (Jiang et al., 2018). Fig. 2G and H showed the red, blue, and yellow fluorescent emissions of La-Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and Y-CDs under a 365 nm laser. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to collect the surface elements of the three CDs. The XPS results are shown in Table S1. Fig. S1 indicates that the Spe-Y-CDs and Y-CDs mainly contained C (285 eV), N (400 eV), and O (531 eV), whereas the La-Y-CDs also contained S (169 eV). This might be a cause for the red shift in the emission spectrum (Liu et al., 2017; Miao et al., 2017). Elemental doping and surface group changes may be the reason for the different fluorescence emission of CDs (Hola et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2019; Zuo et al., 2017). At the same time, the proportion of O elements is much higher. The doping of S element and the increase of carboxyl content may be the reason for the red shift of La-Y-CDs emission. The FTIR results of three CDs showed in Fig. S2, the absorption peaks of La-Y-CDs at 1560 and 1385 cm−1 (the peaks of carboxylate) are much higher than Spe- Y-CDs and Y-CDs also further confirm this conjecture. The CDs showed a wide-angle XRD pattern with a broad peak at 21.0° and 26.6° resulting from the layer spacing (002) of the graphite structure, 0.41 and 0.34 nm (Fig. S3). Due to steric hindrance of the functional groups at the edge of the graphene or the planar distortion of sp3 C in the graphene plane, the increased layer spacing resulted in large broad peaks. The peak at 26.6° is the characteristic diffraction peak of graphite (002) (Ge et al., 2015).

CDs can be applied to combat bacteria due to their highly positive surface and ultra-small size characteristics. The zeta potentials of the three CDs were + 17.06, + 51.20, and + 12.28 mV for the La-Y-CDs, Spe-Y-CDs, and Y-CDs, respectively (Fig. S4). Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and multidrug-resistant Salmonella typhimurium (MRST) were selected to assess the antibacterial properties of the three CDs and their precursors. Fig. 3A displays the antibacterial activity at 25 mg/mL. As shown, the control group and the Y-CDs had no antibacterial properties. Lipoic acid and the La-Y-CDs were slightly bacteriostatic at that concentration, and spermidine and Spe-Y-CDs strongly combated the bacteria. To confirm the antibacterial properties, another five bacterial strains were used to determine the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) value by a standard dilution method of the samples. Fig. 3B showed the MICs of Spe-Y-CDs for the seven bacterial strains. The MIC values were 64 μg/mL for Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas fluorescens, Yersinia enterocolitica, MRSA, and MRST and 32 μg/mL for the other bacterial strains. All MICs of the CDs and precursors are displayed in Fig. 3C. The MIC of Spe-Y-CDs (32 μg/mL) was 781-fold lower than the MIC of spermidine (≈25 mg/mL). The MIC of kanamycin for most pathogenic bacteria is not as low as that of Spe-Y-CDs. Spe-Y-CDs have the same or even better antibacterial effect than traditional antibacterial agents such as kanamycin, their antibacterial ability is more spectral, they have obvious antibacterial effects on drug-resistant strains and will not cause bacterial resistance. The amino groups on the surface, the ultra-high positive charge, and the small particle size of the Spe-Y-CDs contributed to bind to the bacteria quickly (negative surface charge), destroying the surface charge of the bacteria, and then entering the bacteria (Bing et al., 2016; Cui et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the fluorescent antibacterial hydrogel (FAH-gel)
ce:section.ce:para: The FAH-gel was synthesized as described in the Experimental Section. All hydrogels were synthesized in two steps. The monomer, initiator, and cross-linking agent were optimized after synthesizing the CDs. As shown in Fig. S5 A and B, the optimization ratio of each hydrogel is displayed in Table 1. Different CDs were added as fluorescent and antibacterial elements to the AP, LCDs-AP, CDs-AP, and SCDs-AP hydrogels ( Fig. 4A). The colors of the resulting hydrogels were also different with the different CDs added. The 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% SCDs-AP had different Spe-Y-CD contents (Fig. 4B). As the number of CDs added increased, the color of the hydrogel gradually deepened.

The mechanical performance of the SCDs-AP hydrogels was investigated with a texture analyzer (Fig. 4C). The 75% SCDs-AP hydrogel (diameter of 20 mm and height of 20 mm) displayed good recoverability after compressing to 50% strain (Fig. 4C (a) and (b)). This hydrogel had repeatable and long-term adhesiveness to the vails (Fig. 4C (c)) and had good stretchability (Fig. 4C (d)). The detailed data were shown in Fig. 4D and Fig. S6. A negative correlation was observed between hardness and springiness within a certain range. The SCDs-AP hydrogel had the best springiness among the different CD hydrogels (Fig. 4D (a) and (b)), indicating that the CDs significantly improved the springiness recovery of the hydrogel. This may be because the CDs regulate the structure and charge distribution of the hydrogel, thereby improving its springiness. Adding 75% Spe-Y-CDs to the hydrogel resulted in better springiness (Fig. 4D (c) and (d)). The electrostatic attraction formed by the positively charged CD groups and the negatively charged anionic monomer groups (AA) was dominant. This charge enhanced the strength of the hydrogel. The content of positively charged groups in the hydrogel network increased when CD content was > 75%, and the increase interaction between the positive molecular increased, resulting in a decrease in the strength of the hydrogel. No obvious differences in cohesiveness or resilience properties were observed among the different hydrogels (Fig. S6).

The microstructure of the hydrogels was characterized by SEM (Fig. 4E). The different CD hydrogels displayed a uniform microstructure after swelling and freeze-drying, but they differed after adding various CDs. An obvious three-dimensional network structure was observed from the hydrogel. However, AP group showed a denser 3D network with smaller pore size than that of CD hydrogel groups, which is consistent with the lower swelling ratio of AP group than that of other groups. The number of positive charges carried by different CDs was different; therefore, adding high positively charged CDs (Spe-Y-CDs) resulted in improved the ability to neutralize anionic monomers and a denser three-dimensional network structure.

The fluorescent properties of the different CD hydrogels were shown in Fig. 4F. The LCDs-AP, CDs-AP, and SCDs-AP hydrogels fluoresced, orange-red, yellow, or blue, respectively, under 365 nm UV light. The AP hydrogels did not fluoresce. Fluorescent hydrogels are expected to be used in the field of fluorescence imaging due to their excellent fluorescence properties. If they can be used as fluorescent signals to monitor the antibacterial process, it is expected to realize in-situ monitoring of the antibacterial properties of fluorescent antibacterial hydrogels. The swelling behavior of the various hydrogels was investigated in Fig. 4G. The swelling behavior of SCDs-AP hydrogels with Spe-Y-CDs and AP hydrogels without CDs were more stable compared with hydrogels with two other CDs, and the swelling ratio was 4–5 (Fig. 4G (a), (b)). This was consistent with the results of its microstructure, as larger voids absorb more water leading to a higher swelling ratio. The different contents of the Spe-Y-CD hydrogels were also investigated. Swelling behavior increased and then decreased as CD content increased. The swelling behavior of the hydrogels was more stable due to static electricity when CD content was > 75% (Fig. 4G (c), (d)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vitro antibacterial activity and cell biocompatibility of the FAH-gel
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of the four different hydrogels was investigated in vitro with the MRSA and MRST strains ( Fig. 5A). The hydrogel matrix (without CDs) was the control group (AP group), which showed no antibacterial activity compared with the CDs-incorporated hydrogels. The hydrogel matrix used hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction between carboxyl and amino groups to carry cationic CDs. The SCDs-AP hydrogels showed strong antimicrobial activities because of the super antimicrobial activities of the Spe-CDs. Fig. 5 reveals a series of bacterial plates cultured with the different hydrogels. The plates coated with the SCDs-AP hydrogel displayed a clear zone of inhibition after 12 h of culture, whereas there was no significant inhibition area observed in the plate coated with the other hydrogels. The inhibitory zone diameters of the four hydrogels for the MRSA and MRST strains were displayed in Fig. 5B. The inhibitory zone diameters of the SCDs-AP hydrogel for the MRSA and MRST strains were 21.7 mm and 17.7 mm, which was 108.5-fold and 88.5-fold higher than the inhibitory zone diameter of the AP hydrogel, respectively. These results reveal that the SCDs-AP hydrogels effectively inhibited both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. After 7 days of continual incubation, the SCDs-AP showed good antibacterial activity (Fig. 5A). Then, SCDs-AP hydrogel usage and bacterial inoculation concentration were optimized. Fig. S7 A and B showed schematics of the optimization of the antibacterial hydrogels (MRSA and MRST, respectively) in vitro; 24-well plates were used to optimize the antibacterial activity of the SCDs-AP hydrogel. As shown in Fig. S7 C and E, the amount of hydrogel used for MRSA was 75 μL, and the added concentration of the strains that produced obvious bacteriostatic differences was 109 CFU mL−1. The amount of hydrogel for the MRST strain was also 75 μL, but the concentration was 108 CFU mL−1 (Fig. S7 D and F). The SCDs-AP hydrogel had better antibacterial performance against MRSA than MRST, which was consistent with the plate culture results.

The biocompatibility and cell affinity of the SCDs-AP hydrogels were investigated using fibroblasts, which synthesize the extracellular matrix and collagen responsible for wound repair (Gan et al., 2019a). As shown in Fig. 5C, L929 fibroblasts were cultured on the AP, LCDs-AP, CDs-AP, and SCDs-AP hydrogels. No treatment was set as control group. Before the implantation experiment or cell culture, excessive APS and other residues in the hydrogel were removed in 75% alcohol and PBS. The cell adhesion and spreading activities of the hydrogels were observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Fig. 5C). After 3 days of culture, the LCDs-AP, CDs-AP, and SCDs-AP hydrogels showed better cell adhesiveness than the AP hydrogel. The SCDs-AP hydrogel showed the best cell adhesiveness. Compared to the control group, the AP hydrogel was not good for cell adhesiveness. Cell viability was further evaluated by the CCK-8 assay (Fig. 5D). The viability of L929 cells on the LCDs-AP, CDs-AP, and SCDs-AP hydrogels was higher than that on the AP hydrogel due to the CDs and pectin, and the SCDs-AP hydrogel had the best cell viability. These results indicate that Spe-Y-CDs played an important role enhancing cellular adhesion and biocompatibility of the SCDs-AP hydrogel and have potential in vivo applications.

Morphological changes in MRSA (Gram-positive bacteria) and MRST (Gram-negative bacteria) were observed by TEM and SEM after treatment with the AP and SCDs-AP hydrogels to further investigate the antibacterial mechanism. As shown in Fig. 6, MRSA and MRST cells treated with the AP hydrogels were similar to the control group, showing smooth cell walls and sharp edges. The bacteria were growing well and were not damaged or destroyed. In contrast, the cell surfaces of MRSA and MRST were damaged and wrinkled after a 3 h incubation with the SCDs-AP hydrogels, and the intracellular contents of many cells leaked, indicating that the SCDs-AP hydrogels achieved the antibacterial effect by destroying the bacterial cell walls or membranes. The TEM results were consistent with the SEM results. Therefore, the SCDs-AP are useful for antibacterial applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vitro release of CDs in FAH-gel at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: The release of CDs from LCDs-AP, CDs-AP, and SCDs-AP hydrogels was investigated for up to 21 d (Fig. S8 A). The figure showed the cumulative concentration of CD released from the nanocomposite hydrogel in PBS solution at pH 7.4. The release curves of several hydrogels fixed with different CDs were similar. The initial burst release was observed within the first two days and then slowly released from all hydrogels. In the end, the CD release amount and release rate of SCDs-AP hydrogels were slightly better than those of the other two hydrogels. Next, the CD release of the hydrogel at different pH was studied. As shown in Fig. S8 B, about 39% and 36% of CDs in SCDs-AP hydrogel was released within 24 h when the hydrogel was immersed in a pH 5.5 and 8.5 buffer, while the value was only 21% when immersed in a pH 7.4 buffer. As the acidity and alkalinity of the buffer solution increase, the release efficiency of CD increases. Similar phenomena are also observed in the other two hydrogels. The pH has an important effect on hydrogen bonding (Zhao et al., 2020). When the pH is lowered, free hydrogen ions increase, and flexible free hydrogen ions can cover the negative atoms on both sides, resulting in the reduction and rupture of hydrogen bonds; similarly, under extreme alkaline conditions, there are too many hydroxide radicals in the solution and few free hydrogen ions to form abundant hydrogen bonds (He et al., 2020). Therefore, over-acid or over-base will cause the hydrogen bond to rupture, thereby releasing CDs (Bhattacharya et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2018; Mao et al., 2017). The growth of bacteria is known to cause acidification of the medium (Hu et al., 2017; Yan et al., 2015). The acidic environment accelerates the release of cationic CDs, and the released CDs play an antibacterial role.

The pH of the MRSA culture at different culture time and the release of CDs from various hydrogels at MRSA culture for 24 and 48 h were investigated. As shown in the Fig. S8 C, the pH of the MRSA bacterial culture solution first drops and then rises with the growth of the culture time. At the beginning, the bacteria metabolize and decompose glucose, maltose, lactose, and sucrose, producing acid but not gas, resulting in a decrease in pH. Insufficient utilization of sources, bacteria start to use carbon containing nitrogen sources, and at the same time the bacteria die during the proliferation process, which will cause the pH of the culture solution to rise (He et al., 2020). Meanwhile, we tested the release of CDs in the MRSA and hydrogel culture medium at 24 h and 48 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. S8 D, CDs were detected in the culture medium of MRSA and hydrogel, and the number of CDs showed an increasing trend with time. This is because the drop in pH leads to an imbalance of hydrogen bonds and charges in the hydrogel, and CDs are released.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vivo antibacterial activity of the FAH-gel
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of the SCDs-AP hydrogel was further investigated in a rabbit model ( Fig. 7A). Purified SCDs-AP hydrogels were implanted subcutaneously on the backs of rabbits (Fig. 7B (a)). The suspension contained 1 mL of S. aureus (105 CFU/mL) injected in the hydrogel (Fig. 7B (b)). About 4 days after the wound occurred, macrophages, fibroblasts and blood vessels began to appear in the wound to provide growth factors, new extracellular matrix, oxygen and nutrients to promote wound healing (Gonzalez et al., 2016; Liang et al., 2020, 2019). Surgical site photographs were taken 7 days later. As shown in Fig. 7C, the wounds in the AP hydrogel group still had prominent pustules and scars, while the wounds treated with the SCDs-AP hydrogels were almost healed. Then, the surgical site was incised to expose any inflammation and infection under the skin. As shown in Fig. 7D, the AP hydrogel group was filled with suppuration, while the tissues surrounding the SCDs-AP hydrogels were not infected. To future evaluate the anti-infective ability of the SCDs-AP hydrogel, the tissue at the surgical sites was harvested and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) (Fig. 7E). Edematous tissue and multinucleated giant cells were observed in the AP hydrogel group, indicating a severe inflammatory response caused by S. aureus. In contrast, the SCDs-AP hydrogel treatment group had practically no edematous tissue or multinucleated giant cells, and only a few minor infections, and the skin tissue around the SCDs-AP hydrogel was in good condition and well repaired. These results indicate that the SCDs-AP hydrogel is antimicrobial in vivo and could be used to safely treat wound infections.

We synthesized fluorescent antibacterial hydrogels by reacting amino-containing CDs and pectin. The hydrogels exhibited multicolor fluorescence, high adhesiveness, high cell affinity, strong antibacterial properties, and high mechanical strength. A simple hydrothermal method was used to synthesize highly positively charged Spe-Y-CDs. The gels showed higher antibacterial activity performance for non-resistant bacteria and for multi-drug resistant bacteria (MRST and MRSA) due to the interference and breaking effect on the surface charge of the bacterial membranes, which was 781-fold lower than the MIC of spermidine. To further expand the biological applications of antibacterial CDs, fluorescent antibacterial hydrogels based on CDs, pectin, and AA were synthesized. These hydrogels had inherent antibacterial properties due to the typical high positively charged CDs in the network. At the same time, the CDs were well dispersed in the hydrogel, and the prepared hydrogels exhibited improved multicolor fluorescence. The in vitro and in vivo cytotoxicity evaluation revealed that the SCDs-AP hydrogels were highly biocompatible due to the addition of pectin and CDs. In addition, the SCDs-AP hydrogels exhibited high tissue affinity and good cell adhesion properties after adding pectin. These characteristics were further attributed to the interactions between the CD amino groups and the AA carboxyl groups. Therefore, the fluorescent antibacterial hydrogels had long-term immobilized antimicrobial activity, and excellent fluorescent properties, which avoided many of the problems related to the use of antibiotics. The SCDs-AP hydrogels combined antimicrobial ability, fluorescence sensing, good mechanical properties and cell affinity in one hydrogel, which was suitable for biological applications.
